inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text: I can build a gradual, low-risk rebuild toward a spring half — I just need one thing from you first: how much time can you realistically commit each week? Please reply with either sessions per week (e.g., 3 sessions/week) and/or total hours per week (e.g., 4–6 hours/week). If it helps, include which days/times are usually available (evening/weekend). I’ll keep the plan conservative to avoid setbacks once I have that.
outbound.html: <html><body>I can build a gradual, low-risk rebuild toward a spring half — I just need one thing from you first: how much time can you realistically commit each week? Please reply with either sessions per week (e.g., 3 sessions/week) and/or total hours per week (e.g., 4–6 hours/week). If it helps, include which days/times are usually available (evening/weekend). I’ll keep the plan conservative to avoid setbacks once I have that.</body></html>

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Thanks for the context. To proceed safely with a conservative rebuild toward a spring half marathon, I need two specifics before I set your plan:
1) Weekly time commitment: how many sessions per week and/or total hours per week? If helpful, include typical days/times you’re available (evenings or weekends).
2) Exact target race/date for 'this spring': confirm the race and its date (or event_date).
Please reply with those two items. In the meantime, keep the Achilles tightness in check: avoid big increases in volume or intensity, and back off if you notice sharp or persistent tightness or pain. We'll adjust as soon as I have your availability and race date.
Best regards,
Coach
outbound.html:
Hi there,

Thanks for the context. To proceed safely with a conservative rebuild toward a spring half marathon, I need two specifics before I set your plan:

1) Weekly time commitment: how many sessions per week and/or total hours per week? If helpful, include typical days/times you’re available (evenings or weekends).

2) Exact target race/date for 'this spring': confirm the race and its date (or event_date).

Please reply with those two items. In the meantime, keep the Achilles tightness in check: avoid big increases in volume or intensity, and back off if you notice sharp or persistent tightness or pain. We'll adjust as soon as I have your availability and race date.

Best regards,
Coach

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Priority this week: protect the Achilles and keep every session controlled. We’re in a yellow-risk window, so stick to easy effort unless you get a clear all‑good from the body.
Today (2026-03-17) — your next session
- 30 minutes easy. Run at a conversational pace (easy aerobic). Warm up 5 minutes and finish with 3–5 minutes easy walking.
- Keep cadence light and posture tall. If the Achilles feels tighter than your normal baseline, shorten the session or walk segments as needed.
- If you feel any sharp pain, sudden increase in pain, or swelling — stop immediately and rest. If that happens, tell me and consider a physio check.
This week (4 sessions total, arranged around your Tuesday morning constraint)
- Tue (today): 30’ easy — do it later in the day if your morning is busy.
- Thu: Strength — 20–30 minutes focused on glutes, single‑leg balance, core and low‑load leg strength. Avoid heavy calf loading; include gentle calf mobility and isometric calf holds rather than high‑load eccentrics.
- Sat: Recovery session — very easy 20–40 minutes (or cross‑train low impact). Keep it conversational.
- Sun: Easy aerobic — 30–45 minutes steady but comfortable.
Key rules to follow
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy or skip hard work. Do not try to “make up” intensity later in the week.
- No hard intervals while we’re managing the Achilles.
- Support the training with steady sleep, simple recovery (foam roll/massage as comfortable), and consistent hydration.
Quick action items for you
- Do today’s 30-minute easy (later in the day if needed). Monitor the Achilles and stop for sharp pain or swelling.
- After the session, reply with a one-line status: how the Achilles felt (0–10) and whether you completed the run as planned. That lets me adjust the plan for next week.
Keep it conservative this week — the goal is consistent, pain-free progress. I’ll adjust the next build step based on how this week goes.

Keep it conservative this week — the goal is consistent, pain-free progress. I’ll adjust the next build step based on how this week goes.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Here’s your update for this week. We’re staying in the base phase with a conservative load to protect your mild Achilles tightness and keep you moving toward your half marathon on 2026-05-17.
What to expect this week (4 sessions):
- Session 1 (today): Easy aerobic run about 30 minutes. If your morning is busy, you can push it to later in the day. Keep effort easy, cadence relaxed, posture tall, and avoid heavy calf loading.
- Session 2 (this week): Strength work about 20–30 minutes. Focus on glutes, single-leg balance, and core. Avoid heavy calf loading; include gentle calf mobility or isometrics as tolerated.
- Session 3: Easy aerobic run about 20–40 minutes. Stay relaxed and avoid hard efforts.
- Session 4: Mobility work about 10–20 minutes. Emphasize calves, Achilles, hips, and ankles.
Important rules:
- No hard intervals while Achilles is being managed.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and do not try to “make up” intensity later in the week.
- Monitor cadence and posture; support the work with sleep, hydration, and simple recovery habits.
Scheduling notes:
- You have four sessions per week. If any day is tight, you can shift a session by a day or two but keep the load easy and the order intact.
Post-session check-in:
After today’s session, please reply with a one-line status: Achilles pain rating (0–10) and whether you completed the 30-minute easy run.
If you want, you can share your Strava link for a quick form check.
Thanks, and take care.
outbound.html:
Hi there,

Here’s your update for this week. We’re staying in the base phase with a conservative load to protect your mild Achilles tightness and keep you moving toward your half marathon on 2026-05-17.

What to expect this week (4 sessions):
- Session 1 (today): Easy aerobic run about 30 minutes. If your morning is busy, you can push it to later in the day. Keep effort easy, cadence relaxed, posture tall, and avoid heavy calf loading.
- Session 2 (this week): Strength work about 20–30 minutes. Focus on glutes, single-leg balance, and core. Avoid heavy calf loading; include gentle calf mobility or isometrics as tolerated.
- Session 3: Easy aerobic run about 20–40 minutes. Stay relaxed and avoid hard efforts.
- Session 4: Mobility work about 10–20 minutes. Emphasize calves, Achilles, hips, and ankles.

Important rules:
- No hard intervals while Achilles is being managed.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and do not try to “make up” intensity later in the week.
- Monitor cadence and posture; support the work with sleep, hydration, and simple recovery habits.

Scheduling notes:
- You have four sessions per week. If any day is tight, you can shift a session by a day or two but keep the load easy and the order intact.

Post-session check-in:
After today’s session, please reply with a one-line status: Achilles pain rating (0–10) and whether you completed the 30-minute easy run.

If you want, you can share your Strava link for a quick form check.

Thanks, and take care.
